text:
<think>I should start with a broad summary of the impact of the printing press on society.</think>
The invention of the printing press in the 15th century revolutionized the way information was produced and shared.  
<think>Now I should specify how it made books more accessible, supporting my claim about information production.</think>
By making books and written materials much cheaper and more widely available, the press allowed more people to learn to read and access new ideas.  
<think>I need to give a specific example of a societal change, such as the spread of new religious or scientific ideas.</think>
This increased availability of information helped fuel major movements like the Renaissance and the Reformation.  
<think>It would be helpful to elaborate on how it affected authority and power structures.</think>
The spread of printed material weakened the control that religious and political authorities had over knowledge and education.  
<think>I should highlight the long-term consequences for literacy and education.</think>
Over time, the printing press contributed to rising literacy rates and the growth of public education.  
<think>To conclude, I should summarize the overall societal transformation caused by the printing press.</think>
In summary, the printing press transformed society by democratizing access to information, fostering intellectual movements, and reshaping power structures.
